skill.name: monitoring-observability
skill.description: Set up monitoring, logging, metrics, dashboards, health checks, and alerting for application and infrastructure observability.
skill.summary: Build practical observability with Prometheus metrics, Grafana dashboards, structured logs, health checks, and alert rules.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical heuristic combinations, but the code execution hits are markdown code-fence false positives and no malicious intent was found. Real concerns are limited to example patterns that can expose operational or personal data, including user/IP logging, stack traces, health-check errors, and an unprotected metrics endpoint. Publish with a warning that users must restrict telemetry endpoints and scrub sensitive fields before production use.

medium_findings.title: Privacy-Sensitive Fields in Logging Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The logging examples record userId, IP address, user agent, request path, error message, and stack data. These fields are useful for observability but can expose personal data or internal details if copied into production without redaction and retention controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly log identifiers, IP data, user agent data, and error details. The risk depends on deployment controls, but the sensitive logging pattern is directly present.

medium_findings.title: Health Check Can Expose Internal Error Messages
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The health check response includes error.message for database failures. Public health endpoints that return raw dependency errors can leak implementation details useful during reconnaissance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code assigns error.message into the response object and returns that object. This is a clear information disclosure risk if the endpoint is externally reachable.

medium_findings.title: Metrics Endpoint Example Lacks Access Control Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Prometheus metrics endpoint returns application metrics without showing authentication, network restriction, or reverse-proxy protection. Metrics can reveal routes, status codes, traffic patterns, and system behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is shown as a plain Express route and the scrape targets are local examples. The risk is moderate because production exposure depends on deployment topology.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command findings point to fenced TypeScript, YAML, JSON, and plain-text examples in SKILL.md. No Ruby backticks, shell execution, or executable skill script was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown fence delimiters or inline tags, not executable Ruby or shell syntax. The repository contains documentation examples rather than runnable scripts.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The ../ paths are markdown links to related skill documents, not filesystem reads or writes. No code path consumes these links as user-controlled file paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only path traversal sequences appear inside markdown link targets. There is no file access API or execution context associated with these links.

low_findings.title: Environment Reads Are Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: process.env.LOG_LEVEL and process.env.NODE_ENV are shown as normal logging configuration values. They do not read environment files, enumerate secrets, or transmit credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variables are limited to log level and runtime environment metadata. No evidence shows secret access or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs and Example Targets Are Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are documentation references, and the local scrape targets are standard Prometheus examples. The sample IP address is logging data, not a network destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are in a References section and the targets are localhost service endpoints. No code sends data to an external service.

low_findings.title: Template Expressions Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography findings correspond to descriptive text and Prometheus template expressions such as label and value placeholders. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found alert annotation templates and prose at the cited lines, not cryptographic APIs or algorithms. This is scanner pattern noise.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Telemetry Fields in Logs
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include user and request metadata in logs. Users should redact identifiers, IP addresses, headers, and stack traces according to privacy and security requirements.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code sample directly logs userId, IP address, user agent, and error details. These fields commonly require minimization or masking.

dangerous_patterns.title: Detailed Health Response
dangerous_patterns.description: The health check can return dependency error messages. Production health endpoints should return coarse status externally and keep detailed diagnostics internal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The response object includes the raw database error message. Exposure depends on whether the route is public.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable signals before incidents affect users. This skill helps create metrics, logs, dashboards, health checks, and alerts for production services.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to instrument an Express service with Prometheus counters and histograms.
Provides Prometheus scrape configuration and alert rule examples.
Demonstrates structured Winston logging with console and file transports.
Outlines Grafana dashboard panels for request rate, errors, latency, and CPU usage.
Provides a health-check pattern for database and Redis dependencies.
Summarizes golden signals and monitoring best practices for production readiness.
limitations: Examples are templates and need adaptation for each runtime, network, and deployment model.
It does not install or configure Prometheus, Grafana, ELK, or Datadog automatically.
Security controls for metrics, logs, and health endpoints must be added by the user.
It does not provide complete runbooks, on-call policy design, or compliance validation.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Service for Production
use_cases.description: Add request metrics, health checks, dashboards, and critical alerts before release.

use_cases.title: Improve Incident Diagnosis
use_cases.description: Create logs and golden-signal dashboards that help isolate latency, errors, traffic, and saturation.

use_cases.title: Standardize Observability Practices
use_cases.description: Use consistent templates for teams that need common metric labels, alert rules, and dashboard structure.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic Metrics
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a service without metrics.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add Prometheus request metrics to my Express service. Include route, method, status code, and latency tracking.

prompt_templates.title: Create Alert Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design Prometheus alert rules for high error rate, slow response time, service downtime, and high memory use.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Dashboard
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to propose a Grafana dashboard for my API. Focus on traffic, latency percentiles, error rates, and resource saturation.

prompt_templates.title: Review Observability Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across metrics, logs, health checks, and alerts.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my observability plan for production gaps. Identify missing signals, risky logging fields, weak alerts, and endpoint exposure risks.

output_examples.input: I need monitoring for a new API service.
output: A metrics plan covering request count, latency histograms, error rate, and process health.
A Prometheus scrape plan for the application and node exporter.
A dashboard outline with traffic, errors, latency percentiles, and CPU panels.

output_examples.input: Our alerts are noisy and not useful.
output: A reduced alert set focused on high error rate, downtime, and sustained resource pressure.
Suggested severity labels and waiting periods to reduce alert fatigue.
Runbook reminders for each critical alert.

output_examples.input: We need safer logging before launch.
output: A structured logging checklist with service, environment, timestamp, request metadata, and severity fields.
A reminder to redact secrets, tokens, passwords, and unnecessary personal data.
Recommendations for retention, access control, and production log review.

best_practices: Restrict metrics and health endpoints to trusted networks or authenticated internal access.
Use low-cardinality labels and define SLOs before creating alert thresholds.
Redact secrets and personal data from logs, then verify retention and access controls.
anti_patterns: Exposing detailed health checks or metrics endpoints directly to the public internet.
Logging request headers, tokens, passwords, or full error stacks without redaction.
Creating many low-priority alerts that do not require immediate human action.

faq.question: Does this skill install monitoring tools?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and configuration examples that users adapt and deploy themselves.

faq.question: Which monitoring stack does it focus on?
faq.answer: It primarily covers Prometheus, Grafana, structured logging, alert rules, and health checks.

faq.question: Can I use it with Datadog or ELK?
faq.answer: Yes, the concepts transfer, but the included examples are strongest for Prometheus, Grafana, and Winston logging.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning. The skill is documentation, but some examples need production hardening.

faq.question: What should I secure first?
faq.answer: Protect metrics and health endpoints, then review logs for secrets, personal data, and unnecessary stack traces.

faq.question: Does it create complete incident runbooks?
faq.answer: No. It recommends runbooks, but teams must write procedures for their systems and on-call process.
